scope: Hash-scoped correction of the two non-accepted E14.4e dossiers; fourteen accepted manifests remain byte-identical.

revisions.boundaryRationale: March begins with the regional-bank failures and May closes at the Federal Reserve stability assessment. The claim is deliberately limited to policy-assisted containment of broad-market repricing; bank failures, fragile sentiment, funding strains and emergency facilities remain explicit counterevidence.

evidenceItems.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
evidenceItems.summary: The Federal Reserve reports that policy actions and sector resilience led financial markets to normalize and that corporate-bond risk premiums remained near the middle of their historical distributions despite regional-bank stress.

The accessible IMF chapter states that forceful policy responses reduced market anxiety, while also recording fragile sentiment and continuing strains; this supports containment rather than an assertion of system-wide calm.

counterEvidence.summary: GAO documents two of the largest U.S. bank failures, weak liquidity and risk management, emergency guarantees and a new lending facility intended to minimize financial contagion.

revisions.boundaryRationale: May isolates the May 6 flash crash. CPB subsequently measured May world-trade volume growth of 2.3 percent and 5.1 percent momentum, while WTO monitoring found no measurable trade impact from the contemporaneous volatility. The label is therefore negative only for cross-border real transmission, not for market functioning.

evidenceItems.provider: CPB Netherlands Bureau for Economic Policy Analysis
evidenceItems.summary: CPB reports an upwardly revised 2.3 percent month-on-month increase in world-trade volume in May 2010 and world-trade momentum of 5.1 percent, directly measuring continued cross-border real activity during the flash-crash month.

WTO economists raised the 2010 world-trade growth forecast to 13.5 percent after faster-than-expected recovery and reported that merchandise trade rose sharply in the first two quarters, confirming that the May market shock did not interrupt the cross-border recovery.

counterEvidence.provider: U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission and Securities and Exchange Commission
counterEvidence.summary: The CFTC and SEC joint investigation identifies the severe and rapid market disruption of May 6, preserving the underlying broad-market event as counterevidence to the mechanism-specific hard negative.
